title: Fry bake
subtitle: Trinidadian fried breakfast bread
excerpt: Soft, pillowy fried bread rounds made with a simple baking powder dough. The Trinidadian breakfast staple eaten with buljol (salt cod salad), fried egg, or avocado.
sourceNotes: Fry bake is the standard Trinidadian breakfast fried bread, distinct from the Jamaican johnny cake in that it uses baking powder rather than yeast, giving a softer, more immediate result. It is eaten with buljol (a salt cod, tomato, and pepper salad), fried egg, saltfish, or avocado. The dough is very simple; the skill is in controlling the oil temperature so the outside puffs and colours without the inside remaining raw.

content.text: Fry bake is a soft, pillowy disc of fried bread made with baking powder dough. It puffs in the hot oil and is almost hollow inside when you lift it out, which makes it easy to split and fill. The dough takes 10 minutes to mix and 15 minutes to rest, then each batch fries in under 5 minutes.

Oil temperature is the one thing to watch. Too hot and the outside browns before the inside cooks. Too cool and the dough soaks up oil without puffing. A small piece of raw dough dropped in should sizzle and float to the surface straight away. The right heat gives a pale golden colour in 2 minutes per side.
Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Add the {{vegetable-oil}} and rub in with your fingers.
Add 200 to 220 ml of warm water, a little at a time, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. Knead gently for 1 minute. Do not overwork it.
Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces. With lightly oiled hands, flatten each piece into a round about 10 cm in diameter and 1 cm thick.
Heat the frying oil in a saucepan to 170°C. Test with a small piece of dough: it should sizzle and float to the surface immediately.
Fry 2 to 3 fry bakes at a time for 2 minutes per side until pale golden and puffed. Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper. Serve straight away.

items.fix: The oil must be at 170°C before the first batch goes in. Each fry bake should be at least 1 cm thick before frying.
items.cause: Oil temperature too low; dough too thin

items.fix: Lower the heat and fry for a longer time at a lower temperature. Flatten the rounds to 1 cm, not thicker.
items.cause: Oil too hot; rounds too thick
items.symptom: Raw doughy centre despite golden outside

items.fix: Knead for 1 minute only. The gluten should be just developed enough to hold the dough together.
items.cause: Dough overworked; not enough baking powder

items.fix: Drain on a thick layer of kitchen paper for at least 60 seconds. A 170°C oil temperature prevents oil absorption.
items.cause: Oil temperature too low; not drained long enough

content.text: Fry bake is the Trinidadian breakfast, eaten with buljol (a salad of flaked salt cod, tomato, spring onion, and pepper dressed with lime), a fried egg, avocado, or any combination of the three. The hollow centre, created by the puffing dough, makes it easy to fill or to tear open and use as a scoop. On the weekend it extends into brunch; at Carnival it becomes an all-hours food. It is one of the simplest things to cook and, eaten fresh from the oil with a cup of cocoa tea, very good.
